skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate AI video workflows through the inference.sh CLI for text-to-video, image animation, lipsync, avatars, upscaling, foley, and clip merging.
skill.summary: Run inference.sh video models for generated clips, animation, avatars, audio effects, and video utilities.
skill.author: inference-sh-8

security_audit.summary: The audit confirms a high-risk remote installer pattern: the Quick Start pipes a downloaded script into a shell. Most Ruby backtick and weak-crypto static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code spans, model identifiers, and documentation text. The skill also intentionally runs an external CLI and sends prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, and video URLs to inference.sh services.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The Quick Start instructs users to download an installer from https://cli.inference.sh and pipe it directly to sh. This is dangerous because any compromise of the endpoint, transport path, or install script can execute arbitrary code before user review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present in the Quick Start. The surrounding install note mentions checksums, but the one-line installer still executes remote code directly.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Runs Remote AI Workloads
medium_findings.description: The skill is built around infsh app run commands that send prompts and media references to inference.sh applications. This is expected behavior, but it exposes user prompts and media URLs to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples invoke the external infsh CLI with prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, and video URLs. This is core functionality, not hidden behavior, but it is still a privacy and network risk.

medium_findings.title: Additional Skill Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The related-skills section recommends npx skills add commands for several other skills. Installing additional community skills expands the trusted code and instruction surface beyond this package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented recommendations and not automatically executed. The risk depends on user action and the security posture of the referenced skills.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick detections correspond to Markdown fenced examples and inline code spans, not Ruby source execution. No evidence found that the skill itself invokes Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown and the matched backticks delimit examples or inline identifiers. There is no Ruby file or runtime context in the analyzed evidence.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to descriptive Markdown text, including the term SHA-256 in an install note and table content. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are front matter description and a Markdown table header area. They do not implement hashing, encryption, or verification logic.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Expected
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are links to the inference.sh site, hosted images, documentation, and placeholder media inputs. These are expected for a skill that documents a hosted video generation service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or placeholder input examples. No hidden exfiltration endpoint or obfuscated network target was found in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Downloaded Script To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The install command pipes remote content directly into sh. Users should prefer a manual download, checksum verification, and explicit execution step.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present as curl followed by a shell pipe. This is a well-known high-risk installer pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Service Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow runs infsh commands against hosted AI applications. Users should treat prompts, URLs, and generated media as data shared with an external provider.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples consistently call infsh app run with user-provided prompts and media references. This confirms networked processing by design.

content.user_title: Create AI Videos With CLI Models
content.value_statement: Video teams need fast access to many generation models without learning each provider interface. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh CLI workflows for video creation and editing.
actual_capabilities: Lists supported text-to-video models, including Veo, Grok, Seedance, and Wan options.
Shows inference.sh CLI commands for generating videos from text prompts.
Documents image-to-video, avatar, and lipsync workflows using media URLs.
Includes examples for video upscaling, foley sound, and clip merging.
Points users to related inference.sh skills and official documentation links.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, account access, and network connectivity.
Does not generate video locally inside Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
Model availability, pricing, latency, and output quality depend on inference.sh and upstream providers.
Media URLs and prompts may be processed by external services.

use_cases.title: Prototype social video concepts
use_cases.description: Create short clips from prompts, then compare model options for speed, quality, and audio support.

use_cases.title: Animate product or campaign images
use_cases.description: Turn approved still images into motion tests using image-to-video models and simple CLI inputs.

use_cases.title: Build avatar and narration workflows
use_cases.description: Connect portrait images and audio files to lipsync or talking-head video models for presentation drafts.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a first clip
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a short AI video prompt for a product teaser. Use inference.sh and recommend one fast text-to-video model.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an image
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an image URL and want subtle motion. Choose an image-to-video model and prepare the CLI command.

prompt_templates.title: Create a talking avatar
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare an inference.sh workflow for a talking-head video from a portrait URL and narration audio URL.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a full video pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CLI pipeline that creates a clip, adds foley sound, upscales it, and merges it with a second clip.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a five-second forest drone shot for a campaign mockup.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a fast Veo model, writes a concise visual prompt, and gives the matching inference.sh command.

output_examples.input: A user has a portrait image and narration audio for a course introduction.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects an avatar or lipsync model and explains which media URLs are needed before running the CLI.

output_examples.input: A user wants to improve a silent generated clip.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests foley sound, optional upscaling, and a merge step for assembling the final sequence.

best_practices: Review provider terms and privacy rules before sending prompts or media URLs.
Use manual CLI installation and checksum verification instead of piping installers to a shell.
Start with short drafts, then upscale or merge only the strongest outputs.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential footage, unreleased assets, or personal data without approval.
Do not assume every listed model is available, affordable, or suitable for commercial use.
Do not run remote install scripts without inspecting and verifying them first.

faq.question: Does this skill create videos locally?
faq.answer: No. It guides commands for the inference.sh CLI, which runs hosted AI video applications.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What video tasks are covered?
faq.answer: It covers text-to-video, image-to-video, avatar animation, lipsync, upscaling, foley sound, and clip merging.

faq.question: What accounts or setup are required?
faq.answer: Users need the inference.sh CLI, login access, and permission to process the selected media.

faq.question: Is there a security concern?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented installer uses a pipe-to-shell command, and all generation uses an external service.

faq.question: Can I use private media URLs?
faq.answer: Only use private media when your policy allows sharing it with inference.sh and relevant model providers.
